title: Why Power Skills Are the Future of Leadership: AI-Powered Microlearning Insights
guestBio: Ioanna Mantzouridou is the founder of Dextego, a groundbreaking platform that uses AI-powered microlearning to help sales teams develop power skills like communication, negotiation, and emotional intelligence. She brings extensive global leadership experience to her entrepreneurial journey, focusing on reshaping the future of learning through innovative technology solutions.
excerpt: What if the key to leadership isn't just strategy and hustle, but empathy, confidence, and connection? Discover how AI-powered microlearning is revolutionizing the way we develop essential power skills in just 5-minute learning bursts.
metaDescription: Learn how AI-powered microlearning transforms leadership development. Insights on building empathy, confidence & power skills from Dextego founder.
keyTakeaways: Power skills like empathy, communication, and emotional intelligence are becoming more critical than technical skills in leadership roles.
Microlearning allows busy professionals to develop essential skills in just 5-minute daily sessions, making growth sustainable and manageable.
AI can effectively teach human skills by providing personalized feedback and adaptive learning experiences tailored to individual needs.
Systems thinking and intuition are essential leadership capabilities that women often excel at naturally but need to recognize and develop intentionally.
Scaling human connection in digital environments requires intentional strategies and tools that prioritize authentic relationship building.
The invisible leadership work that women do daily—emotional labor, relationship maintenance, and team cohesion—deserves recognition and strategic development.
Building confidence at work is a learnable skill that can be developed through consistent practice and the right learning frameworks.

pullQuotes.text: The key to leadership isn't just strategy and hustle — but empathy, confidence, and connection.

timestamps.label: Ioanna's journey from global leadership to entrepreneurship
Why power skills matter more than ever in business
How AI and microlearning work together
Systems thinking and the invisible work of leadership
Scaling human connection in a digital world

content:
The landscape of leadership is shifting dramatically, and if you're still thinking that technical expertise alone will carry you to the top, you're missing a critical piece of the puzzle. The future belongs to leaders who master what Ioanna Mantzouridou calls "power skills"—those deeply human capabilities like empathy, emotional intelligence, and authentic connection that no algorithm can replicate.

In this transformative conversation, Ioanna shares how her journey from global corporate leadership to founding Dextego opened her eyes to a fundamental gap in how we develop the skills that truly matter. After spending years in executive roles, she recognized that the most successful leaders weren't necessarily the most technically proficient—they were the ones who could connect, communicate, and inspire others with genuine authenticity.

The Revolution of Microlearning

Here's what's revolutionary about Ioanna's approach: she's proving that you don't need hours of training seminars or expensive executive coaching to develop these crucial capabilities. Through AI-powered microlearning, complex skills like negotiation, emotional intelligence, and confident communication can be developed in just five-minute daily sessions. This isn't about dumbing down the learning process—it's about making it sustainable for the reality of your busy life.

The beauty of microlearning lies in its respect for your time and cognitive capacity. Instead of overwhelming you with information you'll forget by next week, it delivers bite-sized insights that your brain can actually process and integrate. When you're juggling leadership responsibilities, family commitments, and personal growth, those five-minute learning moments become powerful catalysts for lasting change.

AI Teaching Human Skills

One of the most fascinating aspects of our conversation centered on how artificial intelligence can effectively teach quintessentially human skills. Ioanna's platform uses AI to provide personalized feedback and create adaptive learning experiences that meet you exactly where you are in your development journey. The AI doesn't replace human connection—it amplifies your capacity for it by helping you recognize patterns, practice responses, and build confidence in real-world scenarios.

This approach acknowledges that developing empathy and emotional intelligence requires practice, feedback, and iteration—just like any other skill. The AI serves as a patient, non-judgmental practice partner that helps you refine your approach until these skills become second nature. It's like having a leadership coach available twenty-four seven, ready to help you navigate complex interpersonal situations with greater wisdom and confidence.

The Invisible Leadership Work

Our conversation took a powerful turn when we addressed the invisible leadership work that women do every day. You know what we're talking about—the emotional labor of maintaining team cohesion, the intuitive reading of group dynamics, the behind-the-scenes relationship building that keeps organizations functioning smoothly. This work is often unrecognized and undervalued, yet it's absolutely essential for organizational success.

Ioanna's insights on systems thinking resonated deeply with this reality. Women leaders often excel at seeing the interconnected patterns and relationships within organizations, understanding how individual actions ripple through entire systems. This capability isn't just valuable—it's becoming indispensable as businesses grapple with increasing complexity and the need for more holistic approaches to problem-solving.

The challenge isn't that you lack these skills—it's that you may not recognize their strategic importance or know how to leverage them intentionally. Systems thinking and intuitive leadership are powerful capabilities that deserve to be developed with the same rigor and attention as any other business skill.

Scaling Human Connection

As our world becomes increasingly digital, the ability to create genuine human connection across virtual platforms becomes a competitive advantage. Ioanna shared valuable insights about how leaders can maintain authenticity and build meaningful relationships even when face-to-face interaction is limited. This isn't about mastering video call etiquette—it's about understanding how to translate your natural leadership presence into digital environments.

The key lies in being intentional about connection. Just as you would deliberately plan a strategy meeting or budget review, scaling human connection requires thoughtful consideration of how you show up, how you create space for others, and how you maintain the emotional threads that bind teams together. Technology should amplify your human capabilities, not replace them.

Building Confidence That Lasts

Perhaps most importantly, our conversation illuminated how confidence—real, sustainable confidence—can be developed through consistent practice and the right learning frameworks. Confidence isn't something you either have or don't have; it's something you build through repeated experiences of competence and growth.

When you combine microlearning with AI-powered feedback, you create a safe space to experiment, fail, learn, and improve. This iterative approach to skill development builds genuine confidence because it's based on actual capability rather than empty affirmations. You become confident because you know you can handle whatever challenges arise, not because someone told you that you should feel confident.

The future of leadership development isn't about choosing between human skills and technology—it's about leveraging technology to amplify your uniquely human capabilities. As Ioanna's work demonstrates, when we combine AI's processing power with human wisdom and intuition, we create learning experiences that are both deeply personal and remarkably effective. The question isn't whether you have time to develop these power skills—it's whether you can afford not to.

quiz.title: What's Your Power Skill Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally lead with power skills and where your next breakthrough might be hiding. Based on insights from our conversation about the future of leadership.

questions.question: When your team is struggling with a challenging project, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Check in with each person individually to understand how they're really feeling about the situation
Step back and look at the whole process to identify what systemic issues might be causing the struggle
Call a team meeting to openly discuss the challenges and create a clear communication plan moving forward
Try to understand the root emotional and practical needs behind the struggle, then brainstorm creative solutions

questions.question: How do you prefer to develop new leadership skills?

options.text: Through real conversations and observing how different approaches land with people in the moment
By learning frameworks and models that I can apply across different situations and contexts
With consistent, structured practice that builds my confidence through repetition and feedback
By exploring innovative methods that combine traditional wisdom with new approaches and technology

questions.question: What type of leadership challenge energizes you most?

options.text: Building trust and psychological safety in a team that's been through difficult changes
Redesigning processes or structures to help an organization run more effectively
Presenting a vision or leading a difficult conversation that could transform how the team operates
Finding ways to maintain human connection and empathy while scaling or going digital

questions.question: When you think about your biggest leadership strength, it's probably:

options.text: Your ability to sense what's not being said and address the underlying dynamics in any situation
Your talent for seeing how individual problems connect to larger patterns and organizational health
Your willingness to have the hard conversations and communicate clearly even when it's uncomfortable
Your skill at understanding what people need emotionally while also pushing for innovative solutions

questions.question: If you had just 5 minutes a day to grow as a leader, you'd want to focus on:

options.text: Practicing how to better tune into and respond to the unspoken needs of your team
Learning new ways to analyze and improve the systems and processes around you
Building specific communication skills that help you show up more confidently in any situation
Exploring how to blend emotional intelligence with new tools and approaches to leadership

questions.question: What frustrates you most about traditional leadership development?

options.text: It doesn't acknowledge or build on the relationship skills and emotional labor you already do naturally
It focuses on individual tactics instead of helping you think strategically about organizational dynamics
It's often too theoretical and doesn't give you enough practice to actually build confidence in real situations
It hasn't kept up with how technology could enhance rather than replace the human elements of leadership

results.title: The Intuitive Connector
results.description: You have a natural gift for reading between the lines and building authentic relationships that others admire. Your leadership style is rooted in genuine connection, and you often know what your team needs before they say it. While you excel at the invisible work of leadership, you might sometimes doubt whether these 'soft' skills are valued enough. This episode will validate your natural strengths and show you how to leverage AI-powered microlearning to amplify your intuitive leadership gifts.

results.title: The Systems Strategist
results.description: You see the bigger picture and naturally think about how all the pieces fit together in your organization. Your power skill is creating order from chaos and helping others understand complex situations. You're probably already doing systems thinking without even realizing it's a valuable leadership capability. This episode will help you recognize how your strategic mindset is exactly what modern leadership demands and give you tools to develop it further through bite-sized learning.

results.title: The Confident Communicator
results.description: You understand that communication is the foundation of great leadership, and you're willing to put in the work to get better at it. Whether you're naturally confident or still building that muscle, you recognize that how you show up matters as much as what you know. You're ready to invest in yourself consistently. This episode will show you how five-minute daily practices can transform your communication skills and help you build the kind of confidence that inspires others.

results.title: The Empathetic Innovator
results.description: You believe that the best solutions come from truly understanding people's needs and experiences. Your superpower is combining genuine empathy with forward-thinking approaches to leadership challenges. You're curious about how technology can enhance rather than replace human connection. This episode will resonate deeply with you as it explores how AI can actually help develop your emotional intelligence and scale the authentic relationships you value most.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manager, the podcast where we reframe invisible labor as the powerful leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, entrepreneur, mother of six and author of The Manager. Today's guest is someone I instantly clicked with at a Tomahawk dinner in New York about a year ago. We ended up talking for three hours about everything from AI to emotional intelligence and I was just beginning to write my book at the time. Since she's read the entire thing and I'm honored to have her here today.

Ioanna is the brilliant founder of DexGo, a platform that uses microlearning to build power skills like empathy, confidence, negotiation, and communication in sales teams. She's multilingual, global-minded, and deeply committed to changing the way we think about learning and human development in the workplace. If you're curious about the intersection of AI, education, and what it really takes to be confident in business, this episode is for you. Hi, it's so nice to have you. Thank you so much for coming on the show, Ioanna.

**Ioanna:** Thank you so much for having me. Lovely to see you always.

**Aimee:** Oh, it's so nice to see you, too. I'm so glad that you had a nice trip, and I'm glad you're back in New York ready to get back on track. So I wanted to start from the beginning with you. Could you take us back to the beginning? How did your background lead you to launching DexGo?

**Ioanna:** Sure. So I think the last almost eight years, I've been very much into psychology, talent development, and really my passion has always been around soft skills. And I always knew that that's what drew me to people. That's what really separates for me the average from the best. No matter what your career is, whether you're a waiter or a CEO or whatever, at the end of the day, the skills that matter are communication, collaboration, leadership. So I've always been thinking about how can we develop these skills and how can we identify these skills in people that we hire and we work with.

For my master's, I studied talent development and strategic management and I worked with a professor who has been researching high potential employees and we had very much the same beliefs. But then we started researching together and I helped him launch his books and truly understood that stars, meaning people like LeBron James, are very rare but we all want them, right? There's no CEO that will tell you I don't hire the best out there. But the question becomes, it's not about how you attract them. It's how you can develop others to become those stars. Otherwise, you're always going to be fighting for a better salary that someone might give them or more perks. It's endless. And on the other hand, when you know how to develop talent, these people become loyal, they grow within your organization and they understand truly the value add that you've brought them.

So while at my previous company Adaptivo, I was working as the VP of People and Chief of Staff and I saw by recruiting and by developing lots of talent that it becomes extremely hard to develop these skills especially in a remote setting because you get to do this through experience, right? When you hire someone they get to see how others in the team operate, learn by their manager through the examples that they see. And now you just give them a screen and you give them maybe an online course and you're like good luck. And I found that very unfair. I do believe employers have a responsibility to develop talent yet they didn't have the tools to do so.

So that's when the idea of DexGo came about - an AI coaching platform for soft skills development that can not replace but really fill in the gap because you don't have access to your manager 24/7. Maybe you're not lucky enough to have a good manager or a safe space to ask questions, but you deserve that place to grow and practice before meeting a client or if you're in a customer success role before being yelled at by an angry customer.

Long story short, it evolved quickly to an AI sales coach where we saw a lot of traction because client-facing roles are the ones that really have to develop these skills to build trust with buyers or else they won't be successful in their roles. And that has now been almost three years that we've had DexGo out and working on adding more and more insights in the platform to personalize the coaching further at scale.

**Aimee:** Wow. Yeah, that's so cool. When we were talking at dinner when I met you about a year ago, you were saying how when you have salespeople in a room and maybe you're doing a sales coaching session that oftentimes the people are afraid to ask questions that would be really important for them to have answered. And having this unique platform where there's no one watching allows your salespeople to ask all the questions that they otherwise wouldn't be confident enough to ask, right?

**Ioanna:** And tailored in a way that you understand it. So I always give the example - some people like a coach that's more direct. Some others like it more confidence boosting, right? When you just have online courses or when you just attend a workshop, it's tailored for the average of the group and that by default is not touching everyone. So now with AI and understanding truly who you're coaching, you're able to speak to the way that they would listen and you can meet them where they are.

Everyone has different skill sets and everyone will apply them differently based on their day-to-day tasks. So let's say I have a call which is a renewal call but you have a discovery call. We need to work on different skills. So that AI coach now integrated to your workspace, to your existing tools, will only keep developing and becoming better as you use it.

**Aimee:** Exactly. Yeah, totally. Which is a good follow-up question to that. So what sparked your belief that power skills like confidence, listening, and empathy are just as important as technical ones?

**Ioanna:** You can quickly tell when you're meeting someone - and I'll continue with the sales example - in the first two minutes if you will ever buy from them or not. And it doesn't matter what they even sell by default. No matter what the technical training they had on the product that they're selling goes out of the room. It doesn't matter. It's irrelevant because if you feel like they're too salesy or too arrogant or they're not listening to you, you're just not going to want to talk to them again.

And although this is so true and we all know it, sales organizations have been so focused on training that's about CRM - like how do I add my deal in the pipeline? What data does it have to have? How do I push it forward? It's really not helpful because you can't expect that you hire an 18, 19-year-old and you give them a phone and you tell them go and talk to a C-suite that they've never talked to before without teaching them these soft skills. And that's why I truly think that it's so important to develop them yet it's so hard because a lot of them are usually developed when you're a kid, right? When you're still in school.

**Aimee:** Oh yeah, absolutely. And I see that with the next book that I'm writing, I definitely feel like there's going to be a lot of that in there as far as teaching self-confidence. There are a lot of things that I learned as a child that I have carried forth into my CEO, my C-suite life, but I actually learned them when I was two, three, four years old.

In fact, my daughter and I were on vacation right now in Hawaii and we were at the pool at a hotel and my husband and I met a man in the pool so we shook his hand and introduced ourselves. And our daughter, our four-year-old went up to the man and said, "Oh, hi. My name is Indie Rose." And she shook his hand and looked him in the eye and shook his hand and I thought, "She's a future CEO. Check her out." But I do think we learn these things - hopefully we're taught as children. If we're not, we do need to be taught in our young adult life as we're developing our careers.

**Ioanna:** Absolutely. And I agree, but the whole point is that you can still develop them even as an adult. And I think what you and I both love with this microlearning is that you can start by just focusing on one thing at a time. We think that oh my god I have this negative trait I'll never change or this person has that they can never change. Everybody can as long as they do one thing at a time and there is this one percent improvement every day and collectively then at the end of the year it is indeed someone else. But of course you cannot let's say score five out of 10 in negotiation and expect tomorrow you're a 10 out of 10 and that's where I think a lot of people lose faith in the process but it's also because a lot of the trainings have not been based on these principles. You can't expect that you'll give someone a course on negotiation which is passive. They'll have to click next next next to complete it and suddenly they'll become expert negotiators. I mean if that was the case, we would all be expert negotiators and work in the CIA.

**Aimee:** Exactly. So this one's a good question for those who aren't familiar. What exactly is microlearning and why does it work so well for building soft skills? Because I think microlearning kind of blew my mind when you explained it to me.

**Ioanna:** Yeah, truly. It is what the word says - micro steps, bite-sized learning every day that ends up collectively teaching you whatever the domain you're learning. And when it comes to soft skills, let's say we're talking about leadership skills for example, what you were saying earlier - you can't expect a 10-year-old to now become the CEO but they're already developing these skills step by step throughout their lives by being in these environments where their skills are being tested then they show up as a leader.

So when it comes to even math, right? We learn a few pieces of geometry, a few pieces of something else over the years we're in school. Similarly with any other topic when you decide to learn it, if you break it down you're going to be able to improve a lot over time. And microlearning is something that has been introduced the last few years in L&D especially in the workplace to be able to fit the busy work day of the employee. Right? We cannot afford to take two hours in a day anymore to learn, but we can afford five minutes prior to a call to do a role play with our AI coach or to read a topic that we're interested in and now apply that in action during the day so you can stick to it, do it again tomorrow, the next day. And that's how we improve through microlearning.

**Aimee:** I love that. So you blend AI with human behavior in such a unique way. How does DexGo use AI to enhance rather than replace human connection?

**Ioanna:** Yes, I love that question because it truly is a misconception that AI can do it all for you and it can automate everything. I truly believe in the power of a human coach plus AI. I think they provide something very different. I think that a human coach cannot by default be in every single meeting you'll ever be, right? They cannot get data from the time they met you till the next time they met you unless you inform them about your progress. So they are lacking that data and analysis but they have much more instinct and they can connect the dots based on their experience and based on patterns that they see from different clients, different organizations, different teams.

The AI though is able to very quickly give you answers, improve as you improve, give you this bite-size training depending on what's your goal every time, but it can't really understand super complex situations. You can programmatically say, okay, if user asks for this, give them the answer. And if we're talking about AI agents, do even more complex stuff. But it still is limited to its knowledge base. You can say a human is limited too. But a human has much more creativity and is resourceful in different ways.

So connecting the two I think is the best way to go about it. And again as I mentioned earlier our platform for example is just filling a gap that the manager is not accessible, that the manager is not approachable and it doesn't mean that the manager is not required.

**Aimee:** Yes. Totally. Because it's not going to be the same five minutes that your manager has available. Right? And how can a manager be available to 200 salespeople at once? It's asking a lot of a manager to have that kind of availability. They're really not. So this gives you a tool to be available when your personnel need the micro education. When they have time to do it, any time of the day, they can hop on and do their five minutes of learning.

And I think that it's just powerful. I think microlearning is powerful not just for sales, but I think it's going to be a powerful tool for humanity in the future for lots of things.

**Ioanna:** One hundred percent. I mean, look at us. We're always on our phone. So having those five minutes instead of scrolling on social media to learn something can be huge.

**Aimee:** I downloaded Smartly and I've started doing that when I feel like, oh, I kind of want to look on social media. I have a few minutes. I'm like, nope, go on Smartly. I've been working on my communication skills just because I've been speaking more and doing podcasts and writing and all of those things. And so it's so neat to have a tool in my pocket that I can learn something new in the 10 or 15 minutes that I have available on that specific day and have it available in all languages. That is another big thing. It's not the same if let's say you're a Spanish speaking person and then your manager doesn't speak Spanish. There are things that can be lost in translation. It's a real thing.

**Ioanna:** So very true. And there's so many companies that are global that have employees in multiple countries who work in multiple languages. I can think of my biggest client - they're global and they're having a huge push in the Spanish language world right now. So they're going to have so many Spanish language employees that need to have access to tools in their own language because when you learn in your own language, it is easier than trying to do it in your second language.

**Aimee:** Yes. One hundred percent. And you feel like you're more safe to fail and learn.

**Ioanna:** Yes. Yeah, that's a great point.

**Aimee:** So what has surprised you most about how people learn confidence and communication in short bursts?

**Ioanna:** So I think what is very interesting is when we have a user who's been doing this for 30, 40 years and then they try it and the before and after is very different. Meaning they will think - because we'll ask them how do you think you'll do on this, they rate themselves then AI rates them and you see immediately that the people who have confidence in their coachability and ability to improve over time will accept that feedback easily. They will question maybe how did they get that calculation - tell me more, I want to understand it better.

But those who lack coachability and have more of a fixed mindset will be very against the result if it's negative for them. If it's a low score - oh this doesn't work or what is this or I don't agree. And it really has surprised me how various people take the feedback and it actually shows that our hypothesis is valid - your potential of being a high performer is very much connected to your coachability and you have to have an open mindset and understand that you do have room for growth in order to want to use a tool like DexGo.

So the impact of confidence in oneself to learning is huge. And on the other hand, if you're not that confident, positive psychology can really help the way you learn because it's another thing for you to practice something and get a response that says you did this wrong, you did this wrong, you did that wrong, okay? Versus you have great potential in this area and this is what you did great, here's how you can improve that. So the way the feedback is delivered is also crucial. It's not just what the feedback is, but it's how you give it.

**Aimee:** That makes so much sense because some people just don't take criticism well or critiquing or being reviewed, but if you can present it in a way that you're offering them a benefit or giving them a confidence boost. I try to do that with my kids all the time, too. If I'm correcting something, I'll try to do it in a very positive way. I never tell them what not to do. I'll tell them what to do instead.

**Ioanna:** There you go. And gamification can really help. Those incentives. I mean, with kids it's the same thing. If you do this right, I'll give you this candy. With us it's you do something else, you'll get confetti and that dopamine will help them get back to it.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. I know Indie has a little book. My four-year-old, she's got a little book and every time she completes an assignment, it has stickers in the back and you get to put a little star on it and that's enough for her. Just little things. People are - we like little rewards. Little dopamine rewards that hey, someone sees us.

**Ioanna:** Yeah. Absolutely.

**Aimee:** In a world of distraction, what do you think actually helps someone retain what they learn?

**Ioanna:** I think it's habit creation. So back to microlearning - making sure you do put on the calendar that hey, I'll practice five minutes a day, 10 minutes a day. As if you are committing to going to the gym for instance, you have to take the same seriousness in your learning to really make it sticky.

I truly think it has to do also with a learning culture that an organization is creating. So for example, you can give them a tool about soft skills but not include soft skills measurements in their performance review because whether we like it or not still people know that they will be measured in certain dimensions at the end of the year and their bonus depends on that. So for them to connect and feel incentivized enough to do it, they need to also see the benefit in regards to that because you can't think that every employee that you have will be self-motivated right? You'll have those which is great but you will primarily have people that need a boost.

So I think it's creating a learning culture internally, making sure you put the time on your calendar for it, and having an accountability partner, whether that's just again an AI thing, maybe sending you a reminder in Slack, or a human just asking you, hey, did you do your 10 minutes a day? That would really help.

**Aimee:** That is so true. Yeah. It's - in scuba diving, you always have a scuba buddy. And in life, it's nice to have an accountability buddy for whatever projects you're working on, whether it's I'm going to do 30 days of yoga in a row or I'm going to write every day for 10 minutes or whatever it is. It really is nice to have a friend come alongside you with that. It would be nice if the organization buddied up their people maybe to make sure that they're doing the work.

**Ioanna:** Yeah. Absolutely.

**Aimee:** I love that. So you have read The Manager and I'd love to hear was there a part that resonated with your personal or professional life?

**Ioanna:** So first of all, I love that it was such a quick, easy read and it really made me get to know you better. But what I enjoyed the most is actually the exercises you have there. And it really made me reflect, not just read, but think about my life. And in particular, you have at page 52 this exercise around creating the vision. The reason why this resonated is because I always think about the vision. That's the way I get motivated. Think of the long term, think of the big picture. And really, it allows me to step out of the daily routine and say, "Okay, who am I? Why am I doing this? What's the impact I'm having?" And then going back to the day-to-day.

So I really like the first question there that says, "If you could do whatever you wanted, what would you do? Where would you go in the world? What would you build or create?" And it made me think how important it is to put aside time to dream because when you say if you could do whatever it's like saying what if you had unlimited budget right? What would you buy? And these questions are very important because they're really a good exercise for creativity because all we tend to do especially as women - we're more prone to negative emotions and we tend to see the scarcity and we tend to see all the challenges that are there that would not allow us to achieve our goal. So you're just flipping that narrative and saying, "Hey, let's think of the what if everything goes well? What if you could do it? What is that thing you want to do?"

**Aimee:** Yeah, using our imagination in a positive way instead of a negative way, right? I learned that in childbirth. I did a course that was hypnosis for childbirth and we would practice using our imagination to visualize the birth going perfectly well. And we would take time every day to do a meditation where we would see the birth going perfectly well because when your brain believes - imagining is just as powerful as experiencing to the mind. The mind actually doesn't know the difference.

**Ioanna:** Right, right and it's visualization that athletes and founders even use across different things and I've tried that too and I tend to do it whenever I remember but my point is that we should actually put it in the schedule too, as with the microlearning - put time for visualization. The neuroscience is there, the facts are there. You cannot question the impact it can have so why not benefit from it?

**Aimee:** Yeah absolutely. My life changed in a big way when I started doing this practice on a daily basis when I really made time for myself to see what I wanted to do next, where I really wanted to go in the long term because then when I get stuck in "Oh my gosh, there's a mess. There's a pile of laundry. The kids are crying and I'm stressed out," it's like take a deep breath, see the future, where we're headed. You know, it's almost like having a heading on your compass and making sure you have a heading on your compass. Make sure you know where you want to go. Because if you're not programming your compass, who is?

**Ioanna:** So I nailed it. Don't forget to program your own compass.

**Aimee:** And then one of the book's themes is systems thinking, especially the invisible systems women run at home and at work. How does this show up for you as a founder?

**Ioanna:** The simple question is okay we have a million things, a million hats we wear as founders and different departments and all that. But I think the deeper meaning that's connected to being a woman has to do with the amount of feelings we pick up, right? Everyone's feelings, everyone's worries, everyone's insecurities that we can pick up because we are empathetic and we know that someone has to take care of that because maybe people don't feel comfortable talking about all this but you can sense it so you feel like you are responsible for it.

It's a challenge and I learned - I would say earlier but not easily - that it's not my responsibility to worry about everyone's feelings but it is my responsibility to create a safe space for them to be expressed if they would like to do that. So I make sure because our team is super diverse, different countries, different languages - it's very, very diverse. So I always want to make sure if someone has something to say, they can say it and if no one asks them that I ask them so they feel seen and that really helps productivity and collaboration a lot. But I think it's also unique to what a female founder can do. I don't really see that in other spaces because it's just something we can pick up easier than others.

**Ioanna:** So what's one microlearning principle women can use today to become more confident in the workplace?

**Aimee:** I think it's choosing the first step. So let's say the goal is I want to speak to boardrooms more. So that's the microlearning principle when you learn. So it's the same thing when you act when you want to let's say be confident to show up and speak to a stranger in a networking event.
